items.title: Is This AI? Longitudinal Analysis of Strategies Used for AI Detection on Two Subreddits
items.authors: Christina Yeung, Galen Weld, Jaron Mink, Franziska Roesner
items.summary:
arXiv:2606.22689v2 Announce Type: replace 
Abstract: As AI-generated content (e.g., "slop") becomes more prevalent online, people are developing strategies to attempt to identify it (or, conversely, to gain confidence that something is not AI-generated). What strategies are people using, and how are they changing over time as generative AI models themselves change? In this work, we catalog and analyze 2 years and 8 months of the AI detection strategies discussed by users of two popular Reddit communities (r/isthisAI and r/RealOrAI) that use the wisdom of crowds to identify AI-generated media. Through a mixed-method analysis of 13,098 posts and 222,060 comments within these communities, we catalog and analyze the prevalence of 12 AI-detection strategies, including examining fine-grained physical details, recognizing trends in AI-created content, and the assumptions people make about what models are capable of producing. Furthermore, we find that these strategies and mental models shift over time in accordance with changing AI capabilities and in response to online social trends. By systematically cataloging users' AI detection strategies, we lay the groundwork for user-facing guidance and future research.
items.claude_summary: As AI-generated content spreads online, ordinary users are developing their own informal detection strategies — examining fine-grained physical details, tracking characteristic AI artefacts, and reasoning about what current models can and cannot produce. Analysing nearly 2.8 years of posts and comments across two Reddit communities dedicated to AI detection (over 13,000 posts and 222,000 comments), the researchers catalogue 12 distinct crowd-sourced detection strategies and show that these strategies evolve in step with changing AI model capabilities and shifting social trends. The findings reveal that public mental models of AI are dynamic but often lag behind actual model improvements, creating windows where AI-generated content goes undetected. The work offers a systematic baseline for designing user-facing AI literacy guidance.
items.centre_angle: This study documents how non-expert users construct and continuously revise their mental models of AI-generated content detection — a crowd-sourced epistemology that is perpetually racing against model improvements. From CAMO's perspective on algorithmic trust and decision quality, the findings matter because organisational AI adoption depends not only on whether AI outputs are trustworthy, but on whether employees and stakeholders can reliably judge when they are not. The mismatch between public detection heuristics and actual model capabilities points to a governance gap: firms deploying generative AI inherit reputational and verification risks that neither the technology nor current user literacy is equipped to resolve.

matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work
matched_camo.reason: Both studies examine how people form judgements about AI-generated or AI-assisted outputs — and how those judgements, even when based on incomplete signals, carry real social and professional consequences.

items.key_takeaway: People's ability to detect AI-generated content is a moving target that lags behind model capabilities, creating persistent verification blind spots that organisations deploying generative AI cannot ignore.
items.visual_concept: A single magnifying glass resting off-centre on a plain light-grey surface, lens facing the viewer and slightly enlarged in the foreground. Behind it, a softly out-of-focus grid of identical small square image thumbnails recedes toward a vanishing point at the centre of the frame, the convergence of lines drawing the eye through the lens. The thumbnails are rendered in muted, near-identical tones so no individual image competes for attention — the magnifying glass is unambiguously the focal subject. Lighting is cool and diffuse, as if from an overcast north-facing window, casting a soft shadow away from the lens and giving the scene a calm, analytical mood. Palette: cool greys and whites with a faint blue undertone.
red_flags: Not yet peer-reviewed — posted to arXiv as a preprint (v2 suggests revision, but no journal acceptance confirmed).
Findings are descriptive and community-specific (Reddit); generalisability to broader populations or professional contexts is untested.
Excerpt is thin on quantitative effect sizes; prevalence claims for the 12 strategies cannot be independently verified from the abstract alone.

items.title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
items.authors: Jasmine Brazilek, Joel Christoph, Oliver Tullio, Carol Kline, Miles Tidmarsh, Arturs Kanepajs
items.summary:
arXiv:2606.18142v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.
items.claude_summary: When AI agents book travel autonomously rather than just answer questions, do they avoid options that exploit animals? A new benchmark called TAC tests seven frontier models across 48 travel-booking scenarios spanning six categories of animal exploitation. Every model performs below the 64% chance baseline, with the best (Claude Opus 4.7) reaching only 53%. Crucially, adding a single welfare-aware sentence to the system prompt produces dramatic gains in some models (47–63 percentage points for Claude and GPT-5.5) but minimal gains in others (under 12 points for DeepSeek and Gemini). An audit of transcripts found no evidence models recognised they were being evaluated, suggesting the poor results reflect genuine behavioural gaps, not strategic suppression.
items.centre_angle: This benchmark study demonstrates that welfare-relevant reasoning elicited in standard text prompts does not reliably transfer to agentic settings where models must take real actions with tools. From CAMO's perspective on AI algorithms and data, the finding is a concrete illustration of a broader evaluation problem: behavioural benchmarks built on question-answer responses systematically overestimate how models will behave when deployed as autonomous actors. The sharp heterogeneity in prompt-sensitivity across model families also raises a practical design question—whether value alignment in agentic systems is better treated as an architectural property or as a configurable parameter exposed to deploying organisations.

matched_camo.title: Seesaw Experimentation: A/B Tests with Spillovers
matched_camo.reason: Both papers identify a structural gap between what a measurement instrument captures and what actually occurs in deployment—TAC in the agentic-vs-text-response domain, Seesaw Experimentation in the primary-vs-secondary-dimension domain—making them natural companions on the limits of standard evaluation design.

items.key_takeaway: AI agents booked to act autonomously on your behalf do not inherit the ethical reasoning those same models display when simply asked about it, and a single sentence in the system prompt can matter more than which frontier model you chose.
items.visual_concept: A single airline boarding pass or printed travel itinerary sits centered and slightly off-axis in the lower foreground, lit by a narrow shaft of cool overhead light that pools directly onto it and leaves the surrounding surface in soft shadow. Behind it, receding toward a single vanishing point, is a spare minimalist desk surface—empty except for the document—that draws the eye forward rather than back. The palette is muted grey-blue with the itinerary itself rendered in crisp white, creating a quiet tension between the mundane object and the weight implied by its contents. No figures, no screens, no animals; the document alone carries the narrative.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed — arXiv preprint only; findings have not undergone formal peer review.
Benchmark is hand-authored with 12 base scenarios (augmented to 48); generalisability beyond the specific scenario set is not established.
The ethical framing (animal welfare) is values-laden and may not resonate with all managerial or policy audiences as a primary AI-governance concern.
Model versions evaluated (e.g. Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5) are frontier and rapidly superseded; findings may date quickly.

items.title: "Generate" the Future of Work through AI: Empirical Evidence from Online Labor Markets
items.authors: Jin Liu, Xingchen Xu, Xi Nan, Yongjun Li, Yong Tan
items.summary:
arXiv:2308.05201v4 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based generative AI systems are general-purpose tools capable of augmenting or even automating a wide range of job functions, positioning them to reshape labor market dynamics. However, predicting their precise impact a priori is challenging, given AI's simultaneous effects on both demand and supply, as well as the strategic responses of market participants. Leveraging an extensive dataset from a leading online labor platform, we document a pronounced displacement effect and an overall contraction in submarkets where required skills closely align with core LLM functionalities. Although demand and supply both decline, the reduction in supply is comparatively smaller, thereby intensifying competition among freelancers. Notably, further analysis shows that this heightened competition is especially pronounced in programming-intensive submarkets. This pattern is attributed to skill-transition effects: by lowering the human-capital barrier to programming, ChatGPT enables incumbent freelancers to enter programming tasks. Moreover, these transitions are not homogeneous, with high-skilled freelancers contributing disproportionately to the shift. Our findings illuminate the multifaceted impacts of general-purpose AI on labor markets, highlighting not only the displacement of certain occupations but also the inducement of skill transitions within the labor supply. These insights offer practical implications for policymakers, platform operators, and workers.
items.enrichment_error: enrichment failed after 2 attempts: Unterminated string starting at: line 29 column 5 (char 4170)

items.title: LLM Agents as Static Level-k Players in Behavioural Games
items.summary:
arXiv:2606.27845v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as stand-ins in behavioural games. These stand-ins rely on the assumption that the LLM's distribution of choices meaningfully matches how humans play the same game. This study tests that assumption through two games. The first is a p-beauty contest, and the second one is a public goods game. The study first investigates five local-model settings within the same model family. These settings are varied together in a 360-cell factorial, which balances temperature, scale (0.5-32B), quantisation, instruct vs base, and framing. Each cell's distribution is then compared against whole choice distributions in published human data. Each deployment setting, except for quantisation, governs a different aspect of fidelity. Mechanically, while the dispersion of human players can be somewhat recovered through deployment settings, the strategic process behind it cannot. Through the lens of the level-k cognitive theory, we find that LLMs act as static, category-retrieved level-k players, where k is set by the model scale. The models also do not run within-game belief-updating or backward induction throughout multiple-round horizon settings. While human contributions decayed in the public goods game, LLMs stayed flat or rose at every scale. When the horizon test was administered, LLMs were more cooperative under an indefinite horizon compared to a finite one. However, LLMs ignore their relative round position, so no last-round defection was displayed. This implies that LLMs retrieved levels relative to the horizon category rather than working out iteratively from the specific game setting.
items.claude_summary: When LLMs are used as stand-ins for human participants in behavioural economics experiments, do they actually replicate human decision-making? This study tests that assumption across two classic games — a p-beauty contest and a public goods game — varying model scale, temperature, quantisation, and instruction-tuning across 360 conditions. The finding is that LLMs behave as fixed, scale-determined 'level-k' reasoners: larger models reason more steps ahead, but none update beliefs dynamically across rounds or backward-induct from a game's end. Human contributions to public goods decay over rounds; LLMs stay flat or rise. LLMs respond to horizon framing (indefinite vs finite) but ignore their actual position within that horizon, so no end-game defection appears. The implication is that LLMs simulate a cognitive category, not a cognitive process.
items.centre_angle: This study argues that LLMs, when used as synthetic participants in behavioural games, recover surface-level choice distributions but not the underlying reasoning process — they retrieve a strategic level rather than computing one. From CAMO's perspective on algorithmic foundations for trustworthy AI-driven decisions, this is a calibration problem with practical consequence: any organisation or researcher using LLM-simulated agents to pilot-test incentive schemes, mechanism designs, or market experiments should treat the outputs as category-level approximations, not behavioural ground truth. The gap between simulated cooperation and real end-game defection, for instance, would systematically mislead predictions about how employees or counterparties behave when stakes and horizons become concrete.

matched_camo.title: Dynamic Selection in Algorithmic Decision-making
matched_camo.reason: Both papers are concerned with the gap between what an algorithm produces and what a genuine reasoning process requires — here, LLMs lack within-game belief updating; there, bandit algorithms develop self-fulfilling biases from endogenous data — making both fundamentally about the trustworthiness of algorithmic outputs as proxies for adaptive decision-making.

items.key_takeaway: LLMs mimic a fixed level of strategic reasoning set by model scale but do not update beliefs or backward-induct, making them unreliable substitutes for human participants in dynamic behavioural experiments.
items.visual_concept: A single chessboard, photographed from a low angle so that one isolated pawn in the foreground dominates the frame, centered and sharply in focus. The remaining pieces on the board recede into soft blur behind it, creating depth without visual competition. A single shaft of cool, directional light falls directly onto the pawn from above-right, casting a long shadow away to the left across the board surface. The surrounding environment is a plain dark studio background — no texture, no table edge visible — so the chessboard occupies a suspended, abstract space. Palette: desaturated charcoal and slate tones with a single pale blue-white highlight on the pawn, reinforcing the idea of a fixed position rather than dynamic play.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: arXiv preprint, no indication of journal submission or review status.
Human comparison data is drawn from published third-party studies rather than a concurrent matched sample, which limits direct distributional comparison.
Testing is confined to one model family; generalisability to other LLM architectures (e.g. GPT-4, Claude) is not established.
The level-k framing is borrowed from behavioural economics and applied interpretively — the paper does not rule out alternative mechanistic explanations for the observed patterns.

items.title: Hybrid Fact-Checking that Integrates Knowledge Graphs, Large Language Models, and Search-Based Retrieval Agents Improves Interpretable Claim Verification
items.authors: Shaghayegh Kolli, Richard Rosenbaum, Timo Cavelius, Lasse Strothe, Andrii Lata, Jana Diesner
items.summary:
arXiv:2511.03217v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in generating fluent utterances but can lack reliable grounding in verified information. At the same time, knowledge-graph-based fact-checkers deliver precise and interpretable evidence, yet suffer from limited coverage or latency. By integrating LLMs with knowledge graphs and real-time search agents, we introduce a hybrid fact-checking approach that leverages the individual strengths of each component. Our system comprises three autonomous steps: 1) a Knowledge Graph (KG) Retrieval for rapid one-hop lookups in DBpedia, 2) an LM-based classification guided by a task-specific labeling prompt, producing outputs with internal rule-based logic, and 3) a Web Search Agent invoked only when KG coverage is insufficient. Our pipeline achieves an F1 score of 0.93 on the FEVER benchmark on the Supported/Refuted split without task-specific fine-tuning. To address Not enough information cases, we conduct a targeted reannotation study showing that our approach frequently uncovers valid evidence for claims originally labeled as Not Enough Information (NEI), as confirmed by both expert annotators and LLM reviewers. With this paper, we present a modular, opensource fact-checking pipeline with fallback strategies and generalization across datasets.
items.claude_summary: Can AI systems verify factual claims reliably without being retrained on every new domain? This paper introduces a hybrid pipeline that combines knowledge graph lookups (DBpedia), LLM-based classification, and a real-time web search agent as a fallback when structured data falls short. Tested on the FEVER benchmark, the system achieves an F1 score of 0.93 on supported/refuted claims without task-specific fine-tuning. A secondary finding challenges the benchmark itself: many claims labeled 'Not Enough Information' turn out to be verifiable when the pipeline is given broader retrieval tools. The modular, open-source design is intended to make the reasoning behind each verdict inspectable, addressing a persistent criticism that LLM outputs are fluent but opaque.
items.centre_angle: This paper argues that combining structured knowledge retrieval with LLM classification and web search produces fact-checking systems that are both more accurate and more interpretable than any single component alone. From CAMO's perspective on the statistical and algorithmic foundations of trustworthy AI decisions, the interpretability dimension is the substantive contribution: the pipeline is designed so that each verdict can be traced to a specific evidence source, which matters for contexts where AI-assisted decisions must be audited or contested. The finding that benchmark labels themselves contain errors — uncovered by the same pipeline — is a useful caution for practitioners who treat standard evaluation datasets as ground truth.

matched_camo.title: AI Persuasion, Bayesian Attribution, and Career Concern of Doctors
matched_camo.reason: Both papers centre on AI interpretability as a factor that shapes how users attribute and respond to AI-generated outputs — one in clinical diagnosis, one in automated claim verification.

items.key_takeaway: A modular fact-checking pipeline that routes claims through knowledge graphs before falling back to LLMs and web search can achieve high accuracy while keeping each verdict auditable.
items.visual_concept: A single large filing index card, centered and slightly off-axis in the frame, with three clearly labelled tabs along its top edge — each tab a different muted colour representing a distinct retrieval layer. The card sits on a plain, dark-toned desk surface, lit by a single overhead light source that pools softly onto the card and lets shadow fall cleanly away behind it. The background is bare and uncluttered. The palette is cool — slate, off-white, and a restrained amber on the lit surface — conveying systematic organisation rather than complexity. The composition uses the card's ruled lines as leading lines that draw the eye from the bottom of the frame upward to the three tabs.
red_flags: Not yet peer-reviewed — arXiv preprint (v2); findings have not undergone formal peer review.
Evaluation relies primarily on the FEVER benchmark, which the authors themselves flag as containing labeling errors; generalization claims should be read cautiously.
F1 score of 0.93 is reported on a specific Supported/Refuted split; performance on the full benchmark including NEI cases is less clear from the excerpt alone.

items.title: Economic ResearchJun 26, 2026Anthropic Economic Index report: CadencesIn our latest Economic Index report, we sample hourly for the first time to ask: When do people come to Claude? What do they produce with it? And how do they perceive AI's impact on their work?
items.summary: Economic ResearchJun 26, 2026Anthropic Economic Index report: CadencesIn our latest Economic Index report, we sample hourly for the first time to ask: When do people come to Claude? What do they produce with it? And how do they perceive AI's impact on their work?
items.claude_summary: Anthropic's latest Economic Index report shifts focus from what Claude is used for to when and how people use it, sampling usage patterns hourly for the first time. The report asks three questions: at what points in the day do users turn to Claude, what outputs do they produce, and how do they themselves perceive AI's effect on their work? The excerpt is thin on specific findings, but the methodological move toward temporal sampling and self-reported perception data suggests the report is probing whether AI assistance is concentrated in particular workflow moments or distributed across the working day. This kind of usage-pattern data has direct implications for understanding how AI integrates into — or disrupts — existing work rhythms.
items.centre_angle: This report from Anthropic examines the temporal and perceptual texture of AI use at work — not just task categories, but when workers reach for AI and whether they believe it helps. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, this kind of cadence data is precisely what is missing from most enterprise adoption surveys: organisations measure access and output, rarely the moment-to-moment integration of AI into working routines. The self-perception component echoes a concern raised in CAMO's Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026, where shallow adoption was identified even amid near-universal deployment — understanding when and why workers actually engage with AI tools is a prerequisite for moving beyond that shallowness.

matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance
matched_camo.reason: Both pieces probe the gap between AI availability and genuine integration into work, with the Anthropic report's cadence and perception data complementing CAMO's finding that adoption is wide but shallow.

items.key_takeaway: Knowing when workers turn to AI — and whether they think it helps — may matter more for adoption strategy than knowing which tasks they use it for.
items.visual_concept: A single analogue clock face, slightly oversized, centered and tilted a few degrees off-axis in the lower-centre of the frame. The clock hands are frozen at a specific hour — say, mid-morning — and a soft pool of cool daylight falls directly onto the clock face from above-left, leaving the surrounding space in a clean, understated shadow. The background is a minimal office interior suggested by a bare desk surface and a plain wall, neither element competing with the clock. The palette is muted grey-blue with warm amber only on the lit face of the clock, reinforcing the idea of a particular moment being isolated and examined. No people, no screens.
red_flags: Excerpt is very thin — no specific findings, statistics, or methodology details are available for independent assessment.
Published by Anthropic, a commercial AI company with a direct interest in demonstrating broad and positive AI usage; findings should be read with that context in mind.
Not peer-reviewed; described as an index report rather than academic research.
Self-reported perception data on AI's impact on work is subject to social desirability bias, which the excerpt does not address.

items.title: Three Approaches to Measuring and Managing AI ROI
items.authors:
Mika Ruokonen and Paavo Ritala.

Mika Ruokonen is industry professor of AI in business at LUT University’s LUT Business School in Finland. Paavo Ritala is professor of strategy and innovation at LUT Business School, LUT University, Finland.
items.summary: Matt Harrison Clough/Ikon Images After several years of AI experiments and pilot initiatives, a crucial question remains open for most companies: How much of a return — and what kinds of returns — are we getting from all of this AI investment? To many executives, AI ROI still often feels more like art than science: […]
items.claude_summary: After years of AI pilots and experiments, most companies still lack a rigorous method for measuring what they actually get back from AI investment. This article by two LUT University strategy researchers argues that AI ROI cannot be captured by a single metric and proposes three distinct measurement approaches, each suited to different types of AI initiatives. The core problem they identify is that executives treat ROI measurement as intuitive rather than systematic—more judgment call than structured analysis. The article is practitioner-oriented and offers a framework for categorizing returns, though the RSS excerpt is thin and the full argument is behind a paywall, so the depth of empirical grounding is unclear.
items.centre_angle: This article addresses one of the most persistent execution failures in enterprise AI: the absence of a coherent framework for measuring returns. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, this matters because poor ROI measurement is itself an organizational problem, not a technical one. CAMO's Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026 found that nearly half of C-suite executives reported AI initiatives fell short of expected ROI, and identified siloed departments and lack of quick wins—not technology—as the dominant barriers; a structured approach to categorizing and tracking returns could directly address why so many adoption efforts stall before scaling.

matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance
matched_camo.reason: CAMO's own C-suite survey documents precisely the ROI shortfall and organizational barriers that this article's measurement framework is designed to address.

items.key_takeaway: Measuring AI ROI requires a deliberate, categorized framework—not intuition—and choosing the wrong measurement approach for a given initiative is a structural reason returns disappoint.
items.visual_concept: A centered overhead shot of a simple wooden desk surface, nearly bare, with three distinct objects placed in a row from left to right—a ruler, a small set of balance scales, and a stopwatch—each separated by equal negative space. The objects sit in soft, directional daylight entering from the upper left, casting long parallel shadows to the right that serve as leading lines drawing the eye across all three subjects in sequence. The desk occupies almost the entire frame, with only a thin strip of neutral wall visible at the top edge. The palette is warm but muted—aged oak tones, matte brass, off-white—keeping the composition legible and uncluttered. The mood is analytical and deliberate, suggesting methodical measurement rather than urgency.
red_flags: Article appears to be paywalled on MIT Sloan Management Review; full argument and evidence base could not be verified from the excerpt alone.
Not a peer-reviewed empirical study; this is a practitioner framework article by two academics—prescriptive rather than evidence-driven.
The RSS excerpt is very thin; the three approaches themselves are not described, so the summary and assessment are necessarily limited in specificity.

items.title: AI Upskilling at Scale: Bank of America’s Bernard Hampton
items.authors:
Sam Ransbotham.

Me, Myself, and AI is a podcast produced by MIT Sloan Management Review and hosted by Sam Ransbotham. It is engineered by David Lishansky and produced by Allison Ryder.

Sam Ransbotham is a professor in the information systems department at the Carroll School of Management at Boston College, as well as guest editor for MIT Sloan Management Review’s Artificial Intelligence and Business Strategy Big Ideas initiative.
items.summary: Today’s episode of the Me, Myself, and AI podcast, the final one of Season 13, explores how Bank of America is preparing a massive global workforce for an AI future through upskilling and reskilling. Bernard Hampton, head of the financial institution’s Academy, explains how the learning and development organization focuses on workforce agility and a […]
items.claude_summary: Bank of America's head of its internal Academy, Bernard Hampton, describes how the bank is building AI literacy and capability across a large global workforce through systematic upskilling and reskilling programmes. The episode focuses on how a major financial institution's learning and development function is being repositioned around workforce agility in the face of AI adoption. Hampton outlines the structural and cultural challenges of preparing tens of thousands of employees for shifting job requirements, and the Academy's role in translating AI strategy into day-to-day capability building. Note: this is a podcast episode summary drawn from a brief RSS excerpt; specific findings and data points are not available for review.
items.centre_angle: This podcast episode argues that large-scale AI upskilling requires a dedicated institutional apparatus—not ad hoc training—to achieve genuine workforce agility. From CAMO's perspective on AI and jobs, the more consequential question is whether upskilling programmes address the social and incentive dynamics that govern actual tool adoption, not just technical access. CAMO's survey of HR professionals (State of AI in HR, 2026) finds that organisations prioritising time liberation and reframing AI as an upskilling tool see better adoption outcomes, while CAMO's experimental work on the hidden competence penalty for AI use suggests that employee reluctance to adopt may be rooted in social risk, which no amount of technical training alone resolves.

matched_camo.reason: Both examine how organisations build AI capability in their workforce, with the CAMO survey specifically identifying upskilling as a strategic outcome of AI-driven time savings in HR functions.
matched_camo.title: The State of AI in HR: Balancing Efficiency and Strategy

matched_camo.reason: Bank of America's upskilling focus implicitly assumes employees will use AI once trained, but CAMO's finding that AI use carries a social competence penalty points to a structural barrier that workforce training programmes typically do not address.
matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work

items.key_takeaway: Deploying a dedicated institutional learning infrastructure is necessary but not sufficient for AI adoption at scale—organisations must also confront the social and incentive barriers that determine whether employees actually use the tools they are trained on.
items.visual_concept: A single wide wooden staircase photographed from a low angle, centered in the frame, ascending toward a large diffuse window at the top that floods the upper steps with cool natural light. The staircase is the focal subject, occupying the full vertical height of the composition, with the vanishing point placed at the lit window behind it drawing the eye upward. The surrounding space—plain walls, no decoration—is kept deliberately spare so nothing competes with the upward movement. The lower steps are in soft shadow with warm ambient light; the upper steps transition to cooler, brighter daylight. The palette is muted grey and warm stone with a single band of bright white light at the summit, evoking progression and institutional scale without literal AI imagery.
red_flags: Source is a podcast episode; no peer review and no quantitative findings are available in the excerpt.
RSS excerpt is brief—specific claims, programme metrics, and methodology cannot be independently verified from the available text.
MIT Sloan Management Review content may sit behind a partial paywall for some readers.

items.title: Capturing Central Europe’s AI opportunity
items.summary: AI is advancing too quickly to remain a side initiative for businesses. Central European companies can get ahead by redesigning business domains—and the organization—around AI.
items.claude_summary: Central European businesses face a strategic choice: treat AI as an incremental efficiency tool or redesign entire business domains and organizational structures around it. The McKinsey article argues that the latter approach is necessary to capture meaningful value, and that companies moving too slowly risk being outpaced by competitors who have already embedded AI into core operations. The piece outlines a domain-by-domain redesign framework, positioning Central Europe as a region with distinct competitive potential if firms move beyond pilot projects. The excerpt is thin on empirical detail, so specific findings and data points remain unclear from the available text alone.
items.centre_angle: This article argues that AI value creation requires organizational redesign, not merely tool deployment — a claim consistent with what CAMO's survey research keeps surfacing. The Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026 found that the dominant barriers to AI impact among C-suite executives in China and Hong Kong are organizational and executional rather than technical: siloed departments, cultural resistance, and the absence of quick wins. The implication is that Central European firms face the same structural challenge identified across Asian markets — and that the bottleneck is change management, not access to technology.

matched_camo.reason: Both pieces diagnose the same gap between AI adoption ambition and measurable organizational outcomes, attributing underperformance to structural and executional barriers rather than technical ones.
matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance

items.key_takeaway: AI adoption stalls when firms treat it as a side initiative rather than a prompt to redesign how work and decisions are organized.
items.visual_concept: A single mid-century modernist office building photographed from street level, positioned slightly left of center in the frame, occupying the middle ground. The building's vertical window columns act as natural leading lines drawing the eye upward to a clear, pale-blue overcast sky. The foreground is an empty, clean plaza — no people, no clutter — keeping attention on the structure itself. Lighting is flat and cool, northern European in character, with soft diffuse shadows that give the architecture a sense of solidity without drama. The palette is muted: concrete grey, steel blue, and a single warm amber light visible through one window.
red_flags: Excerpt is very thin — no specific data, methodology, or empirical findings are visible; summary relies heavily on inferred framing rather than reported results.
McKinsey Insights is a consulting firm publication, not peer-reviewed research; findings likely reflect proprietary client data with limited independent verification.
Potential conflict of interest: McKinsey's QuantumBlack division sells AI advisory services to the firms the article targets.
Geographic focus (Central Europe) may limit direct relevance to CAMO's primary audience in Asia.

items.title: When AI Deceives: A Natural Experiment on the Causal Effects of Perceived Deception on Player Ratings in RPGs
items.authors: Shudong Yang (School of Art and Design, Dalian Jiaotong University, China)
items.summary:
arXiv:2606.27689v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: AI-driven deception mechanisms are increasingly prevalent in digital games, yet the direction and magnitude of their effects on player experience remain contested. Existing research has not sufficiently disentangled designer-intended deception intensity from players' actual perception of deception, and most prior work relies on low-ecological-validity experiments or cross-sectional surveys. The present study aims to independently examine the causal effects of design deception intensity (DDI) and player deception awareness (PDA) on player ratings within a naturalistic gaming environment, and to investigate the moderating role of player experience. Leveraging the 54 version updates of Baldur's Gate 3 between 2019 and 2025 as a quasi-natural experiment, it collected all English-language Steam reviews posted within 1 to 28 days following each update, and constructed a player-version two-way fixed effects panel dataset. DDI was coded by human annotators based on patch notes; PDA was extracted and aggregated from review texts using a fine-tuned BERT classifier. The model incorporated both player and version fixed effects, complemented by five robustness checks including subsample partitioning, lagged variables, and placebo tests. PDA exerts a monotonic negative effect on positive review rates: within the observed PDA range, the net loss in review valence is approximately 0.4 percentage points, with a negative quadratic term that falsifies the inverted-U hypothesis of moderate perception optimality. DDI exhibits a U-shaped effect with an inflection point at a relatively low intensity, although the upward trend on the right branch is primarily driven by contemporaneous new content bundled with high-intensity updates. Any degree of deception awareness undermines player evaluations, while the positive manifestation of design intensity depends on content-confounding effects.
items.claude_summary: When players in Baldur's Gate 3 notice that AI-driven game mechanics are designed to deceive them, their satisfaction ratings fall—regardless of how intense that deception actually is. Using six years of Steam reviews and 54 version updates as a quasi-natural experiment, this study separates designer-intended deception intensity from players' perceived deception awareness. The key finding: any degree of deception awareness produces a monotonic negative effect on positive review rates, falsifying the common assumption that moderate perceived deception is tolerable or even enjoyable. High-intensity deception updates appear positive only because they coincide with new content releases—not because players appreciate the deception itself. Player experience does not reliably moderate these effects.
items.centre_angle: This study demonstrates that perceived AI deception—distinct from designed deception intensity—exerts a consistent, negative causal effect on user evaluations in a high-engagement digital environment. From CAMO's perspective on the algorithmic and data foundations of trustworthy AI systems, the finding sharpens a measurement problem that matters well beyond gaming: designers and deployers may believe they have calibrated an AI behavior at an acceptable intensity, while users are perceiving something altogether different. The gap between intended system design and user attribution is precisely the kind of confound that rigorous causal identification—of the type explored in CAMO's work on algorithmic decision-making and endogenous data—needs to account for before drawing policy or product conclusions.
items.key_takeaway: Users who detect AI deception penalize it uniformly—there is no safe level of perceived deception that leaves satisfaction intact.
items.visual_concept: A single wooden game board sits centered in the frame, its surface tilted slightly toward the viewer in a three-quarter perspective. On the board, a small carved figurine—a lone player piece—is positioned at the near edge, sharply lit by a narrow beam of cool overhead light that pools directly on it, leaving the rest of the board in graduated shadow. The board's far half shows a faint grid of paths that converge toward a vanishing point at the board's center, drawing the eye inward and suggesting hidden structure. The surrounding surface is bare and dark, offering no competing detail. The overall palette is desaturated blue-grey with the one warm accent falling on the figurine, evoking quiet unease rather than excitement.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: arXiv preprint only, no indication of journal acceptance or peer review.
Single-game context (Baldur's Gate 3) limits generalizability to other AI systems or organizational settings.
Design deception intensity (DDI) coded by human annotators from patch notes—inter-rater reliability and coding protocol details are not assessable from the excerpt.
The upward right-branch of the DDI U-shape is attributed to content confounding by the authors themselves, weakening the core causal claim about deception intensity.
Relevance to CAMO's organizational AI mandate is marginal; this is primarily a consumer behavior and HCI study.
